title: quera.com/blog
why:
- Quantum computing is not ready for immediate large-scale production, but it offers real scientific and strategic value through pilot projects and co-design engagements.
- Current quantum systems do not yet meet operational requirements such as repeatable results, auditable provenance, multi-tenant scheduling, and cost-predictable throughput expected by HPC centers for production workloads.
- Hands-on experience with quantum problem formulation, algorithm design, and hybrid integration is valuable now, as it builds long-lead-time skills that position organizations to capitalize quickly when fault-tolerant quantum systems arrive.
- Metrics like qubit quality and coherence, error rates, routing overhead, system uptime, and compiler maturity are more indicative of practical readiness than raw qubit counts or isolated "advantage" demonstrations.
- Early engagement in workforce development, algorithm co-design, and hybrid workflow integration enables organizations to be better prepared for future advances in quantum hardware.
